title: Adding, the Hard Way
lesson:
So how do you do useful math on a machine that can't add? With a little algebra. Subtraction plus negation gives you addition for free:

    a + b  =  -((-a) - b)

Read it from the inside out: start with negative a (LDN of a), subtract b to get negative a minus b, which is the negative of (a plus b). Store that, then load its negative to get a plus b back. Three of the Baby's seven instructions (LDN, SUB, STO) plus a scratch word in memory are all you need.

This is the same kind of thinking that lets a modern CPU build multiplication out of shifts and adds, or build every logic gate out of NAND. A small, well-chosen instruction set is a starting kit, and everything else is just a combination of those pieces. The Baby is the purest example of that idea in the whole history of computing.
prompt: Work out 6 plus 9 using only LDN, SUB, and STO (there's still no ADD). Leave 15 in the accumulator, then STP.
starterCode:
; a + b is the negative of (-a - b). Use a scratch word 't' for the middle step.
        LDN a      ; A = -6
        SUB b      ; A = -6 - 9 = -15
        STO t      ; t = -15
        LDN t      ; A = 15
        STP
a:      NUM 6
b:      NUM 9
t:      NUM 0
hints: Start with negative a: LDN a gives you A = -6.
SUB b gives you A = -6 - 9, which is the negative of (6 + 9). Store it, then LDN it to flip the sign back.
successText: A must equal 15 (that's 6 plus 9) at the stop.
reference:
LDN a
SUB b
STO t
LDN t
STP
a: NUM 6
b: NUM 9
t: NUM 0
